stories.title: Truist Championship Round 1 Underway — McIlroy Seeks Unprecedented 5th Quail Hollow Title

x_posts.label: Post 1
x_posts.text:
Rory McIlroy is back at Quail Hollow — and it might as well be his living room.

4 wins. 5 top-10s. Set the course record twice. Won his first PGA Tour title here.

Round 1 is underway. He's chasing a 5th — something no one has ever done. #TruistChampionship

facebook.long_form:
Rory McIlroy is back. And he's back at the one course where he is, without question, the most dominant player in PGA Tour history.

Quail Hollow Club in Charlotte is where McIlroy won his first PGA Tour title in 2010 — setting a course record of 62 as a 20-year-old and beating Phil Mickelson by four shots. He came back in 2015 and broke his own record with a 61 in round three, then won by seven shots. He won again in 2021, beating Abraham Ancer by one. And in 2024, he closed with a 65 to beat Xander Schauffele by five.

Four wins. Five other top-10 finishes in 14 starts. A course record he holds twice.

Now, coming off back-to-back Masters victories — which have only been achieved by three other players in history — McIlroy is at Quail Hollow seeking a 5th title. One that no player has ever won at this event.

Round 1 of the Truist Championship is underway today. The defending champion is on the course. And there's nowhere in golf he'd rather be.

Who do you have winning the Truist Championship this week? 🏌️

article.headline: McIlroy Returns to His Happiest Hunting Ground — Can He Win a Record 5th Quail Hollow Title?

stories.title: LIV Golf Virginia Opens — Rahm Leads Standings by Over 300 Points, Seeks Third 2026 Win

x_posts.label: Post 2
x_posts.text:
LIV Golf Virginia is underway at Trump National.

Jon Rahm leads the individual standings by 302 points. He's already won twice in 2026.

Can Bryson DeChambeau — coming back from a wrist injury — push back?

#LIVGolf #LIVVirginia

facebook.long_form:
Round 1 of LIV Golf Virginia is underway at Trump National Golf Club in Potomac Falls, Virginia — and the eyes of the LIV world are on Jon Rahm.

The Spanish superstar has been the story of the 2026 LIV Golf season. He won in Hong Kong. He won in Mexico City. After four events, he leads the individual standings with 779 points — a 302-point gap over second-place Bryson DeChambeau.

DeChambeau arrives at Virginia managing the aftermath of a wrist injury that forced him out of the Mexico City final round. He's cleared to play and is expected to be at full strength, but after a month away, the question is whether he can immediately challenge Rahm's pace.

Trump National Golf Club is a power-friendly design by Tom Fazio — stretching to 7,679 yards along the Potomac River, par 72. Distance off the tee matters, which plays into both Rahm and DeChambeau's strengths.

Can anyone stop Rahm from a third 2026 LIV title? Round 1 will give us early answers.

Who wins LIV Virginia — Rahm, DeChambeau, or a surprise? ⛳

article.headline: Rahm Leads LIV Individual Race by 302 Points — Virginia Is His Chance at a Third 2026 Win

stories.title: Three Runner-Ups and One Big Prize Waiting — Scheffler Targets Aronimink Redemption

x_posts.label: Post 3
x_posts.text:
Scottie Scheffler has finished 2nd in three straight PGA Tour events.

Lost to McIlroy at the Masters. Lost in a playoff at the Heritage. Lost by 6 at Doral.

He's the world No. 1. The PGA Championship is next week. Something has to give. #PGAChampionship

facebook.long_form:
Let's talk about Scottie Scheffler — because what he's been doing for the last month deserves a real conversation.

The world No. 1 finished runner-up at the Masters, losing to Rory McIlroy. Came back the following week and lost a playoff to Matt Fitzpatrick at the RBC Heritage. Then closed out April by finishing 13-under at the Cadillac Championship — six shots behind Cameron Young.

Three consecutive runner-ups. The PGA Tour confirmed it is the first time any player has done this since Sergio Garcia in 2014.

On paper, this is a story about near-misses. But step back and look at what it actually means: Scheffler has been top-2 at three consecutive PGA Tour events, meaning he has been the best or second-best player in the field at every start. The consistency is extraordinary. The conversion rate just hasn't matched it — yet.

Next week is the PGA Championship at Aronimink Golf Club. Scheffler is the heavy favorite. The course is a Donald Ross design that rewards precision, elite ball-striking, and course management — three things Scheffler does better than almost anyone in the world.

If there's a course designed to end the streak and deliver a major, Aronimink is it.

Is this Scheffler's week to break through at the PGA Championship? 🏆

article.headline: Three Straight Runner-Ups and One Big Prize Ahead: Scheffler's Road to Aronimink

stories.title: Nelly Korda Is Four Points from LPGA Hall of Fame History

x_posts.label: Post 4
x_posts.text:
Nelly Korda has 23 of 27 points needed for the LPGA Hall of Fame.

She's 4 away. She has 3 wins and 3 runner-ups in 6 starts in 2026.

The last person to do that was Annika Sorenstam — in 2001. #LPGA #NellyKorda

facebook.long_form:
Four points. That is all that stands between Nelly Korda and a permanent place in the LPGA Hall of Fame.

In a season that has been historically dominant from the first tee shot, Korda now has 23 of the 27 points required for induction. She has won three times in 2026 — at the Hilton Grand Vacations TOC, the Chevron Championship, and the Riviera Maya Open at Mayakoba — and finished runner-up in the other three events she has entered.

That is three wins and three runner-ups in six starts. Nothing worse than second place. The last player to start an LPGA season with that kind of consistency was Annika Sorenstam in 2001 — arguably the greatest women's golfer who ever lived.

The historical context only deepens. Korda is now the youngest player since Nancy Lopez in 1980 to reach 18 career LPGA victories. With four more HOF points needed, two more wins this season could put her in the Hall.

This is a historical moment happening in real time — and it's only May.

Are you watching Nelly Korda's 2026 season closely? When do you think she gets inducted? 💫

article.headline: Four Points from Forever: Nelly Korda Is Closing In on the LPGA Hall of Fame

stories.title: The Aronimink Deep Dive — What Donald Ross's Masterpiece Means for the 2026 PGA Championship

x_posts.label: Post 5
x_posts.text:
Donald Ross designed Aronimink Golf Club and called it "my masterpiece."

The last time the PGA Championship was played there, Gary Player won — in 1962.

It returns next week (May 14-17). Three par-3s over 210 yards. It won't be easy. #PGAChampionship

facebook.long_form:
One week from today, the most historic course in modern major championship golf opens its gates for just the second time.

The PGA Championship begins May 14 at Aronimink Golf Club in Newtown Square, Pennsylvania — and it will be the first time this legendary Donald Ross design has hosted the tournament since Gary Player won there in 1962.

That gap — 64 years — is remarkable. A course that its own architect described as "my masterpiece" has been waiting since the Eisenhower era for its moment back on the biggest stage.

Ross completed Aronimink in 1928. It features what experts consider the most elaborate bunkering of any of his designs — which is saying something, given that Ross is responsible for some of the most revered layouts in golf history. Three of the course's par-3s measure over 210 yards. The greens are undulating and demanding. Errant shots do not get away cheaply.

Scottie Scheffler (+350) is the betting favorite, and it's easy to see why: Aronimink demands the kind of precision ball-striking and course management that defines his game. Rory McIlroy, fresh off back-to-back Masters victories, will be among the top contenders as well.

An estimated 200,000 spectators are expected to attend. For the first time in six decades, the world's best golfers will try to conquer Donald Ross's masterpiece.

Who wins the 2026 PGA Championship at Aronimink? 🏆

article.headline: Donald Ross Called It His Masterpiece. 64 Years Later, the PGA Championship Returns to Aronimink.
